data.title: My Outlook For The Market In 2026: An Optimistic But Cautious Stance
data.description:
I am finishing writing an article about how I increasingly like American assets and want to increase my exposure to companies and ETFs such as the S&P 500 (SP500, SPY, IVV) and the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). And this is despite the fact that I am Brazilian and have a bit of a home bias (which I have to fight against). As soon as this other article is published, I will leave the link in the comments if you are interested in reading it in its entirety.

But in short, the reason is quite simple: I believe that the American market is a structural play, and that every investor in the world should be positioned in these assets for the long term, since this is where risks are usually very well rewarded and there are potential positive surprises along the way.

Conviction is also super important in this scenario. Believing that companies will actually deliver growing earnings and still have potential for optionalities helps build optimism.

But as the title says, I am also cautious about the outlook for 2026. This is because returns, mainly in the short term (1 year is short term for me), do not depend only on company earnings, but also on some macroeconomic factors, capital flow, and valuation. As the equity risk premium is not the best at the moment, I expect a timid market performance for 2026, unlike the +17% YTD return of the S&P 500 or the 22% return of the Nasdaq that we have seen so far.

Good Companies, Good Surprises

Before discussing some factors that will/may impact market returns in 2026, I need to bring up the main point, which is companies, because as a fundamental analyst, that is my focus. As mentioned, the United States has the best companies in the world, and that really deserves a premium, not only in valuation but also in conviction.

Think about it: bad companies usually bring negative surprises, or rather, they bring problems to their investment cases. The opposite is also true. Good companies bring good surprises. This is one of the main points for a structural “buy rating” for the QQQ and S&P 500. In the long run, companies like Amazon (AMZN) have prospects of compounding EPS at a very interesting pace for shareholders, because not only should they continue their brilliant execution and gain market share, but they are literally creating new markets and sources of revenue. Think about what they did with the developing of AWS, which came from an internal problem and became one of the main pillars of the AMZN's case. I believe that over time they should also do this with robotics, for example.

In short, these companies are innovating and investing heavily in new technologies (not just AI, although I believe this will be a key point for performance next year), and should continue to add a lot to the index and compounding its EPS.

But since we are talking about 2026, the main point is the consolidated earnings. Thinking about the entire index, how much do you think the EPS of the major companies will rise?

Remember that these are the S&P 500 holdings:

Well, with so much weight on companies that are growing absurdly, with 10 large companies representing 40% of the index, it's not crazy if you said a high number, above double digits.

Remember I mentioned that the S&P 500 had a total return of ~17% so far? In September, that number was closer to 12%, and most of the return was related to earnings growth, with the rest coming from multiple expansion, according to Nationwide Investment Research and Morgan Stanley (MS).

Now, considering that the return has already risen and the valuation of some companies important to the index has increased since September, such as Apple (AAPL), which rose more than 20%, it is possible to infer that this equation now depends a little more on multiple expansion, let's say 50/50, about 8% earnings growth and about 8% multiple expansion (and the rest from dividends).

Thinking about the first part, which is earnings growth, do you think companies can replicate this in 2026? Personally, I believe so. Nvidia (NVDA), which is the largest weight in the index, for example, has estimates of EPS growth of ~59% in fiscal year 2027 (close to calendar year 2026). This figure is higher than the estimates for fiscal year 2026.

Well, Nvidia is not the exception among the leading names in the S&P 500. The market expects that, on average, the leading stocks will grow EPS by ~24% (simple mean) in 2026 (close to that, since it is a proxy considering some fiscal years).

Let's make a conservative assumption that the rest of the index (60%), which includes oil companies that have suffered this year, consumer companies that have also suffered, and that next year will not see a dramatic recovery, and that the rest of the index will have earnings growth of approximately 0%. If 40% of the index grows 25% YoY and the rest does not grow, we are still talking about 10% EPS growth for the S&P 500, which seems pretty solid to me, and also a 'skeptical' assumption, giving us more margin of safety.

What I mean here is that even though this depends on macroeconomic factors, and whether or not we will see a more challenging scenario, as long as companies like Nvidia, Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple continue to generate cash flow, make buybacks, and grow their EPS significantly, this should boost the S&P 500's earnings growth and sustain another year of solid returns.

AI, Macroeconomic Scenario, and Valuation

But the problem, as I mentioned, especially in the short term, is that it's not just earnings growth that matters. Valuation matters too.

Let's take the TTM P/E as a proxy. Over the past three years, Nvidia and Amazon have seen a contraction in multiples, which can be explained by a number of reasons, such as concerns about Amazon, but also the maturing of these companies. Both Nvidia and Amazon now deliver very solid margins and have reached a greater stage of maturity than three years ago, so this is priced into expectations.

But note that for Apple, Microsoft, and Alphabet, the multiple has increased considerably over the past three years. What strikes me most is that Apple's multiple has increased so much, even though the company is struggling to achieve higher topline growth than its peers in a sustainable manner, though it is still a very healthy company.

This already makes me a little apprehensive, since if market sentiment changes, whether in relation to AI or some macroeconomic factor such as capital flow, these multiples could normalize, causing a drop in the S&P 500.

And that's because I haven't even mentioned companies like Palantir (PLTR), which now occupy a more relevant position in the index and also have astronomical multiples.

I can even see a scenario where these companies maintain their multiples at the same level next year, but that will depend not only on whether they deliver in the quarters, but also on whether they can keep AI-related expectations so high throughout 2026. In my view, 2025 was a year of greater optimism, while in 2026 the market will need more evidence. For example, for Apple to remain at this level, in terms of multiples, as an analyst, I could only justify it if I saw it delivering better results and proving that it can (significantly) increase its margin with automation and AI, as well as that its investments are bearing fruit. This is also true for many other companies.

The impact of macroeconomic factors such as tariffs and tax cuts from the Big Beautiful Bill, as well as lower interest rates, will be important, which, together with fiscal impulses, such as Trump's talk of a $2,000 “dividend” per person, may have a positive effect, especially for consumer companies. But at the same time, there is the fiscal counterpoint, as US debt is already high. In addition, it is worth remembering that 2026 is a midterm election year, which could increase volatility. In other words, for the macroeconomic landscape, I think complexity is likely to continue throughout the coming year, and what I expect is uncertainty.

If we add uncertainty about AI, a macroeconomic scenario that may be positive but is still unclear, and already high multiples for several companies, I do not expect another big multiple expansion for the main U.S. indexes. In fact, I believe in a 'healthy' contraction, with some companies maintaining similar multiples (deservedly so), while others, such as Apple, correcting slightly, and on average, a slight multiple contraction.

Basically, these uncertainties related to AI and macroeconomics are the risks for a larger correction in multiples, which could greatly affect my stance. For instance, if earnings growth is 8%, but a multiple correction in the consolidated is strong, such as 20%, we are already talking about a 12% drop. Even if it seems unlikely, it is still a possible scenario.

Little Contraction + Earnings Growth = Mid Single Digits Returns

I, like anyone else who tries to predict a scenario with so many variables, will probably be wrong. But my intention here in giving a price target for the S&P 500 in 2026 is not to be exactly right, but rather to have a direction and know how to position myself in it.

But let's put together what I've mentioned throughout the article: solid earnings growth from major companies, in the high single digits or low double digits, along with a slight contraction in the multiple. I expect earnings growth to remain solid, near 10% or even a little above.

More specifically, I believe in a scenario such as: 10% earnings growth, but with a 5% contraction in the consolidated multiples, which would give a return of 5% per year. Rounding up, I expect the S&P 500 to be close to 7,200. A timid year, especially compared to the good returns of 2025, but in my view, a solid enough return to remain cautiously optimistic.

It is interesting to note that this makes me a more skeptical analyst compared to the rest of the market. From what I have seen, most forecasts from major banks such as J.P. Morgan (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) are around 7,500, often mentioning interest rate cuts and AI-related earnings growth. As I mentioned, my intention is not to be exactly right, but rather to know what is likely to impact the future so that I can position myself, and I believe that an optimistic but cautious stance is ideal for the coming year.

In particular, my actions are, at least so far, positioning myself in some stocks that I consider more asymmetric, remaining optimistic about some tech cases such as Amazon and Alphabet, but at the same time accumulating cash.

data.title: Amazon: Do Not Underestimate The Catch-Up Trade (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) offers investors two ways to win from generative AI. The company’s cloud computing division has seen accelerating demand, and I expect that acceleration to continue moving forward. The company’s e-commerce business already offers top-tier shipping speeds at robust profit margins, but I expect margins to expand as AI and robotic automation boost efficiency even further. The stock valuation looks very reasonable, and I see substantial upside based on my thesis for margin expansion. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

AMZN Stock Price

I last covered AMZN in August, where I rated the stock a "Buy" due to it “devouring the physical and digital worlds.” The stock has fallen slightly since, but this ignores a brief run-up following a strong quarterly report.

The volatility has given investors an opportunity to invest alongside AWS momentum at former prices. Some investors might find it notable that AMZN is one of the few of the “Magnificent 7” to not have delivered strong results over the past year. That underperformance might be a cause for opportunity.

AMZN Stock Key Metrics

In the most recent quarter, AMZN delivered $180.2 billion in revenue, exceeding guidance of $174 billion to $179.5 billion.

The company saw operating income come in flat YoY at $17.4 billion. This was right around the midpoint of guidance of $15.5 billion to $20.5 billion. This was unusual given that the company has been delivering its stronger beats on the bottom line. Some investors might gloss over this as just being more evidence of the rising costs of AI, but that would be incorrect. The company notes that the quarter saw a $2.5 billion charge related to a "legal settlement with the Federal Trade Commission” as well as a $1.8 billion charge related to estimated severance expenses. Excluding these items, operating income would have come in at $21.7 billion, surpassing the high end of guidance.

The company continued its strength in North America, with sales rising 11% YoY. Operating income was down for the reasons just discussed above. The company has been able to continually take market share in part due to its best-in-class logistics infrastructure, all while generating expanding profit margins (after excluding the FTC settlement charge).

The company also saw some strength abroad, with sales growing 14% YoY or 10% on a constant currency basis. I expect the company to drive international margins higher to be more in line with North American margins over time. The company benefits from being able to repeat its proven playbook while having superior financial resources to the competition.

AMZN saw its cloud computing division, Amazon Web Services ("AWS"), revenue growth accelerate to 20% YoY, reflecting its largest growth rate in 11 quarters. Operating income jumped slower, but I expect margins here to also move sharply higher over the long term.

Looking ahead, management guided the fourth quarter to see $206 billion to $213 billion in revenue, reflecting 13% YoY growth at the high end, and $21 billion to $26 billion in operating income.

On the conference call, management noted that the backlog came in at $200 billion, with some deals occurring after the quarter in October, which “together are more than our total deal volume for all of Q3.” Like the other hyperscalers, I expect AWS revenue growth to continue accelerating higher over the near to medium future. Management did appear to confirm this, noting that they think they “can continue to grow at a clip like this for a while.”

Management called out adding groceries to same-day deliveries as being a key catalyst, noting that grocery customers “are visiting the site more often and returning twice as often as non-perishable shoppers.” Management discussed their well-publicized reorganization, noting that their goal is to operate “like the world’s largest start-up,” and to that aim, “it's important to be lean, it's important to be flat, and it's important to move fast.”

Is AMZN Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

For those not following this story closely, it may be a good time to point out that AMZN is not a bubble - it is far from it. The stock recently traded at around 30x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for substantial operating leverage as the company sustains double-digit top-line growth moving forward. That growth outlook looks reasonable if not conservative given the likely acceleration that may take place at AWS.

There are two important and visible catalysts here. The first is an acceleration in growth rates at AWS, which is already underway. Analysts expect AWS to see growth accelerate into the 20% range next year. That projection looks supported given the company’s heavy and continued investment in CapEx. I should acknowledge that the company is not having as much headline fanfare as Alphabet (GOOGL), as investors are unlikely to get so excited about innovations in the Trainium chips versus the TPU chips. I suspect that this has a large part to do with GOOGL having their consumer-facing Gemini product. Even so, the numbers speak for themselves, and AMZN benefits by not having exposure to the potentially threatened search business.

The other catalyst is margin expansion, which I expect to occur from both AWS and, more crucially, the e-commerce division. Reports indicate that AMZN already has plans to replace as many as 600,000 jobs (including future jobs) with robot automation. I expect AWS to reach 40% net margins over the long term, if not higher, largely due to operating leverage. I expect e-commerce margins to move sharply higher as the company executes on the aforementioned automation initiatives, increases its mix of third-party sales, and continues to invest in its logistics infrastructure. Furthermore, I note that the company’s best-in-class shipping speeds are arguably the e-commerce business’ biggest moat, and the company has been able to take a cut of sales outside of Amazon.com, including Tiktok. I expect the company to be able to even increase commission rates over the long term. These lead me to expect margins for e-commerce to hit the double digits, but I use 9% for now. Based on a 30x earnings multiple (or 12x sales multiple) for AWS, we arrive at a $1.9 trillion valuation. That multiple looks conservative given the 20% projected top-line growth and implied 1.5x price-to-earnings growth ratio ("PEG ratio"). Based on a 30x earnings multiple for the e-commerce business, we arrive at a $1.7 trillion valuation. This suggests a fair value of around $330 per share.

AMZN Stock Risks

It is possible that the tariff dispute will escalate again. Some reports indicate that roughly 50% of the seller population is based in China. It is still unclear if the rise of generative AI will benefit all hyperscalers, or if this will be a “winner takes most” kind of market. Governments might enact regulation to weaken the company’s hold on the e-commerce market, perhaps requiring it to separate out its logistics arm.

AMZN Stock Conclusion

AMZN’s struggling stock price does not do justice to the strong momentum it is seeing in both of its core businesses. It has finally become a real AI winner at AWS, and I expect the coming years to allow it to benefit from its dominance in e-commerce. The stock valuation looks too conservative in light of the clear paths to margin expansion. AMZN is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: VMBS: NAV Probability Shift
data.description:
The Vanguard Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF (VMBS) is a way to gain exposure to the US mortgage market in a packaged, liquid form backed by public agencies. This ETF invests most of its assets in mortgage-backed securities (MBS), which are issued by three major players in the US mortgage system: (1) Ginnie Mae, a federal agency that offers an explicit guarantee backed by the full faith and credit of the US government, and (2) Fannie Mae and (3) Freddie Mac, which are government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs). In all three cases, investors receive a guarantee of timely payment of principal and interest, but in the case of Fannie and Freddie, this guarantee is corporate (from the GSE itself), not a direct obligation of the Treasury, although the market treats them de facto as quasi-sovereign risk.

As we will see throughout the analysis, I currently recommend a qualified Buy. I believe that with the rate cuts we are seeing and that the market is advancing or discounting for 2026 (moderate cycle of cuts), they may cause a drop in the yields of the MBS held by the fund, causing the prices of the bonds themselves to rise and leading to an appreciation of the ETF's NAV. We qualify this scenario, as the cycle of cuts must be gradual because if it is abrupt, mortgage prepayment situations may occur, which would shorten the duration of the ETF, limiting the price increase of the MBS themselves.

The Strategy

The fund's strategy is relatively easy to understand, as it aims to replicate the performance of the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Float Adjusted Index, a market-weighted index of intermediate-maturity agency mortgage bonds. To achieve this replication of the index's performance, the fund managers use the “index sampling” replication approach, which consists of taking a representative sample to replicate the main characteristics of the index, rather than replicating it security by security. The manager is committed to investing at least 80% of the fund's assets in bonds that are part of the index or have very similar characteristics, maintaining a weighted average life consistent with that of the benchmark.

ETF Profile

Analyzing the ETF's profile, we can see that it has the price structure of a fixed-income vehicle. Over the last 12 months, the price has moved 1.97%, forming a narrow range between $44.45 and $47.48, which indicates low volatility, a normal attribute for this type of ETF. We can see that the ETF pays a dividend of $1.82, with a TTM yield of 3.87%. The dividend is payable monthly, and with these figures, we can say that a large part of the fund's return currently comes from the coupon, not from capital appreciation, which is consistent with our thesis of potential revaluation of the fund in an environment of gradually falling interest rates.

The Portfolio

In the previous snapshot, I present some relevant data from the VMBS portfolio. We can see that it is a vehicle with an intermediate duration and an average coupon of 4%, which is very much in line with what its benchmark provides. The current fund portfolio consists of more than 1,400 mortgage bonds, which, with a duration of 5 years, place it in the middle of the curve in terms of interest rate sensitivity, which is where we want to be to capture upside if the Fed gradually lowers rates.

In the second image, we can see how the fund is distributed in terms of credit quality. According to the previous photo, this is a high-quality income vehicle, with almost 100% in government debt, since mortgages securitized by government agencies are considered almost like sovereign risk.

The distribution by maturity helps us understand the origin of the ETF's intermediate duration. We can see in the image that there is practically no paper below 1 year (0.10%) and the bulk of the fund is concentrated in the 1–5 year (32.7%) and, above all, 5–10 year (66.05%) tranches, with barely 1.15% between 10 and 15 years.

The Peers

Looking at the panel of VMBS competitors, it can be seen that in terms of fees, the ETF analyzed is one of the cheapest, with an annual expense ratio of 0.03%. On the one hand, we have ETFs such as VMBS, SPMB, and MBB, all of which are agency MBS tracker funds with low fees (0.03-0.08%), and on the other hand, we see other multi-sector or actively managed products, such as JMBS, DEED, and VABS, which range from 0.22% to 0.75% in expense ratios in exchange for taking on slightly more risk outside of agency MBS portfolios.

If we look at the one-year return chart, the result is quite revealing. The VMBS, SPMB, MBB, and JMBS funds are practically neck and neck, all obtaining around 6–6.5% total return, while the most expensive fund, DEED, only contributes a few extra tenths of a percent, and VABS even lags behind despite its greater flexibility in terms of portfolio composition. This indicates that, at least in the last year, paying more for active management or greater exposure has not guaranteed a performance premium over VMBS, which uses indexing.

Macro Overlay

To conclude my analysis, I propose the chart above, which shows the real yield on the 10-year bond compared to the monthly return on MBS. Looking at this chart, we can see that every time real rates fall, MBS enjoys months of positive gains, thus benefiting VMBS. The key point for the reader is that, if the 2026 cycle of cuts materializes in a moderate way, it is reasonable to expect a controlled fall in real yields and, with it, a positive environment for VMBS due to both a fall in yields and a compression of spreads. I believe we will see this situation sooner rather than later, given the Fed's recent comments and because the inflationary problem stemming from the pandemic has not yet been resolved, meaning the Fed will not risk making aggressive cuts. If this comes to pass, and you allocate 2-3% of your portfolio to this ETF, you are likely to see your investment grow within 18 months in my view. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Kevin Hassett As Fed Chair: Bond Vigilantes Are Warning Us
data.description:
President Trump will announce the new Fed Chair early next year, but the market is pretty sure it will be Kevin Hassett, the current Director of the National Economic Council. Before Thanksgiving, the probability for both Hassett and Waller to replace Powell was 35%, now only Hassett stands at 85%.

At this point, it seems very likely that Hassett might be the next Fed Chair, but what does this mean for financial markets?

The signal from the bond vigilantes

Over the last few months the stock market experienced a volatile and confusing period, but it was clear about one point: rate cuts are appreciated.

As you can see, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) started to drop from mid-November, which coincided with a more hawkish stance from the Federal Reserve. Back then, the probability of a rate cut in December was about 60%, so rather uncertain. Now, after more dovish statements from the FOMC members, the CME FedWatch Tool signals an 87% probability of a rate cut next week.

In other words, as the rate cut probability got higher, the S&P 500 Index recovered almost all the November losses. This is strong evidence that the stock market wants lower rates to keep climbing, and from this perspective, Kevin Hassett seems the right man to rely on.

Hassett’s vision is aligned with that of U.S. President since he is in favor of lowering rates in order to boost the economy.

I think that the President thinks rates could be a lot lower, and I agree with him on that.

Hassett in an interview with Bloomberg.

In comparison, Powell seems much more hawkish. In fact, Hassett believes the Fed should lower rates by 50 bps over the next meeting. Therefore, from May 2026, I wouldn’t be surprised if the Fed takes on a different approach regarding monetary policy. QE might come back and the Fed Funds Rate might even reach 3% or even lower.

At first glance this seems bullish for the stock market, but sometimes the latter tends to be shortsighted. Sure, lower borrowing costs might increase GDP growth, however, we are totally overlooking the elephant in the room: the inflation rate is already 40-50% higher than the target.

Easing too much the monetary policy means risking a higher inflation and a significant dollar devaluation. The bond market is typically more effective in anticipating the trajectory of the economy, and it is currently signaling a lack of trust toward the potential new Fed stance.

The 10Y Treasury Rate (US10Y) was close to break below the 4% threshold; the 30Y Treasury Rate (US30Y) was heading to 4.50%. Since Hassett’s odds of becoming Chair increased, US Treasury Bond Rates started to rise once again.

Bond Vigilantes are sending a clear message: too many rate cuts are not allowed, otherwise the long-term maturities will be sold. This would lead to a paradoxical situation: short-term rates decline in line with the Fed Funds Rate, but long-term rates spike as they are disconnected from Fed policy. Furthermore, lowering interest rates too much has also consequences on exchange rates.

As you can see, the Dollar Index showed the same trend. Following the increased probability of Hassett as Fed Chair, the US Dollar (DXY) has depreciated against major foreign currencies.

Conclusion

The stock market is celebrating a more dovish Fed, and Hassett seems willing to give to the market what it wants. The economy will benefit from lowering rates over the short-term, but Bond Vigilantes are suggesting otherwise.

The main risk is that this approach will lead to a second inflation wave in the second half of 2026, therefore they are selling long-term bonds. Plus, a high inflation will probably devaluate the dollar and the Dollar Index already presented signs of it. Needless to say, if Bond Vigilantes are right, the stock market would face a significant crash.

On November the S&P 500 dropped 4% in 10 days just because a 25-bps rate cut was not certain anymore. I think this event puts in perspective what would happen if the Fed would be forced to increase interest rates because of a rising inflation: a year like 2022, but probably worse.

In your opinion who is right? The stock market or Bond Vigilantes?

data.title: The Trade Desk: Bargain EBITDA Multiples As Margins Recover
data.description:
We're poised to exit 2025 at record highs for the stock market, forcing many value-oriented investors like me to think about deep rotations in our portfolios for next year. In my view, 2025 has been a market characterized by big winners and losers, and I'm surprised by the speed at which many former market darlings have converted into value stocks. These names, in my view, offer incredible rebound opportunities for next year.

The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD), one of the largest digital ad exchanges in the industry, has fallen by roughly two-thirds since the start of the year. Yes, we acknowledge that the company's growth rates have materially decelerated, owing to the company's own execution stumbles. At the same time, we do have to wonder: at what point is the risk fully priced in?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on The Trade Desk in October, when the stock was trading near $50 per share. I'll acknowledge that playing the rebound on this stock has been a trade that requires a lot of patience, and I didn't expect the stock to continue falling despite a very strong recent Q3 earnings print. That said, amid a visible recovery in margins and ultra-low valuation multiples, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this name.

Since valuation is now the No. 1 reason to invest in The Trade Desk, let's start with an overview of the stock's latest multiples against Wall Street's updated expectations for the following year. At current share prices near $39, The Trade Desk trades at a market cap of $18.74 billion. After we net off the $1.44 billion of cash on The Trade Desk's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $17.31 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, The Trade Desk has guided to $375 million of adjusted EBITDA on $840 million of revenue (+13% y/y, or 18% y/y excluding last year's political advertising), which implies full-year EBITDA and revenue of $1.17 billion and $2.89 billion, respectively.

For next year FY26, Wall Street is estimating The Trade Desk to hit $3.35 billion in revenue, or 16% y/y growth. If we conservatively assume a flat 40% adjusted EBITDA margin on next year's revenue profile, adjusted EBITDA in FY26 would be $1.34 billion. This positions The Trade Desk's valuation at 12.9x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA - needless to say, a bargain low-teens multiple in an expensive stock market, especially for a high-margin business that is still growing its top line at a high-teens pace. We note that The Trade Desk historically traded at mid-teens multiples of revenue instead of EBITDA.

There are positive fundamental considerations for investors here as well. While we note that The Trade Desk faces competition from the likes of Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) in facilitating digital ad buys, and that the company's rollout of its latest Kokai ad-buying platform has been riddled with missteps, the bottom line is that the company is still estimating the worldwide ad market to total up to a staggering $1 trillion. More and more advertising will shift toward programmatic, data-driven purchases, which is what The Trade Desk specializes in. Also, expertise in video advertising is becoming of critical importance as brands increasingly look to capture younger generations' attention through the likes of TikTok Shop. The Trade Desk can claim tremendous domain expertise here, with roughly half of its ad spending conducted in the video channel.

From an investment perspective, we also like The Trade Desk's clean balance sheet, which it's putting to use via buybacks. The company recently expanded its buyback program by an additional $500 million, which represents roughly 3% of the company's current market cap—a great way to take advantage of falling share prices. The company also generates consistent adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow to keep financing further shareholder returns.

All in all, while I think The Trade Desk deserved a correction this year on slowing growth, the slide has deepened to an irrational point. Investors should take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Download

Let's now go through The Trade Desk's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

The Trade Desk's revenue grew 18% y/y to $739.4 million, which came in well ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $719.5 million (+15% y/y), and the company's own guidance of $717 million.

We note that the company's growth rate largely kept pace with 19% y/y growth in Q2. In addition, The Trade Desk is also facing one-time headwinds from political advertising activity last year, which typically produces a large amount of non-repeating election cycle messaging and a tough comp for this quarter. Excluding political ad spending last year, the company notes that growth would have been even stronger at 22% y/y.

It's important to reflect on the fact that The Trade Desk's challenging Kokai rollout and sales missteps aren't the only variable in the equation. The fact that The Trade Desk is generating ~20% y/y growth amid a tough macro landscape in which many consumer brands have pulled back on advertising is quite impressive.

We like the fact that The Trade Desk brought in a new Chief Revenue Officer alongside a new Chief Operating Officer, and the company is making wholesale changes to its go-to-market organization to improve sales traction. Per CEO Jeffrey Green's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Since March, we've welcomed a new COO, a new CFO and a new CRO, who we just announced last week and began this week. Our COO, Vivek Kundra, has already driven operational progress and continues to improve our operations. Under his leadership, we've brought greater structure, discipline and clarity to how we operate, streamlining our go-to-market organization, improving coordination across regions and teams and instilling stronger operating cadences that enhance accountability and performance.

While we've always been data-driven in running campaigns and servicing our customers, we're building a more data-driven culture that emphasizes measurable outcomes for our employees and consistent execution. These changes are helping us scale more predictably and efficiently while fostering a culture of ownership and operational excellence that will serve us well in the years ahead.

Specifically, the company is re-evaluating its sales incentives and compensation structure to better incentivize its sales force. The company is also keen to grow overseas, where the international share of total spending on the Trade Desk's platform is currently only at 12%.

The company's new CFO Alex Kayyal, a former partner at Lightspeed Venture Partners, also noted that The Trade Desk expects to gain market share in 2026 ahead of the TAM growth in global advertising.

On the profitability front, we note that The Trade Desk's adjusted EBITDA grew 24% y/y to $317.5 million, with adjusted EBITDA margins rising to 43%. This is a 2-point y/y improvement, and a 4-point sequential improvement versus a 39% margin in Q2, where margins had briefly fallen on a y/y basis.

Management's guidance for Q4 implies even further margin leverage to a 45% adjusted EBITDA margin in Q4. Specifically, management is focused on reducing duplicate roles, especially in its sales organization, where it found that it had multiple salespeople covering the same accounts. These efforts may have the dual benefits of driving cost savings as well as improved sales execution.

Key Takeaways

Despite its many challenges this year, The Trade Desk is still part of the fundamental fabric of how ads are purchased and shown across the internet today. Though competition from large tech conglomerates certainly is present, the company still expects to gain share in a $1 trillion advertising market. Look past near-term, macro-driven headwinds to build a position in this stock while it's trading at bargain levels.

data.title: Mastercard: Premium Payments Business, But Returns Likely To Mirror The Market
data.description:
Mastercard (MA) remains a market-leading financial services business that has been steadily growing at a double-digit pace, representing a top- and bottom-line story so far. MA operates at market-beating margins, has a moderately leveraged capital structure, and has a significant moat, fueling a bullish narrative.

The stock trades at an above 33x earnings multiple, a 3x premium vs. peers at 11 forward P/E. The stock has risen by 3% over the past 12 months and underperformed the benchmark. An elevated valuation and weak stock price performance cloud the outlook, and it may be challenging to justify a significant multiple expansion without unlocking a new catalyst.

I rate Mastercard a Hold with a $611 price target over the next 12 months. This presents a 12% upside possibility, but market-like performance. I think MA remains well positioned to appreciate in the stock price following bottom-line growth, but I'm not sure whether it will be enough to outperform the benchmark in the short term. I favor a consolidation stance.

FQ3 2025: The 20th Consecutive Double-Beat Quarter

On Oct. 30, the company posted FQ3 2025 results. This was another solid quarterly report for the company, indicating excellent execution and underlining the 20th consecutive double-beat quarter. The market reacted to the results with a roughly flat, 0.31% decline in the stock price over the following trading session.

Mastercard delivered $8.60 billion in revenue, implying a nearly 17% growth on a year-over-year basis, and roughly $70 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $4.38 in diluted EPS, a 13% surge on a year-over-year basis vs. $3.89 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.07 above analysts' expectations.

In the earnings presentation, management reported $5.14 billion in adjusted operating income, an 18% surge over the past 12 months. MA has also generated $3.46 billion in adjusted operating expenses, indicating a 15% increase on a year-over-year basis. I think this remains impressive, underscoring that MA continues improving margins, and it achieved 59.8% in adjusted operating margin in FQ3 2025 from 59.3% in the same quarter last year. In addition, this highly supports a bullish narrative for the company, underscores significant market presence, and continues steady performance.

The payments processing company achieved $2.75 trillion in GDV, highlighting a 9% growth on a year-over-year basis. Although this may appear incredible, I also think that it slightly contributed to the bearish narrative and fueled consolidation. The company experienced only a 7% growth in the US, reaching $819 billion in GDV, while the rest of the world posted $1.93 trillion in GDV. Mastercard is trading at a significant premium vs. peers, and such metrics cloud the future outlook. It remains a high-quality business, but it also seems that the market already knows it. If GDV accelerated, I think this could meaningfully influence sentiment.

On the upside, management highlighted that the business operates on all cylinders. The payment network posted $5.18 billion in revenue, a 12% growth on a year-over-year basis. The value-added services and solutions delivered $3.42 billion in top-line contribution, a significant 25% increase over the past 12 months. I think this supports a bullish stance. The company has become more diversified, and demand in its value-added segment indicates a high-quality solution. It covers services such as data protection, tokenization, fraud prevention, and others, which remain core services, arguing that the upward trend is likely to continue.

Mastercard experienced an expense increase. Although it may be concerning, the margins are expanding, and fundamentals are improving. The general and administrative expenses remained the highest contributor, reaching $2.92 billion in the quarter, reflecting a 14% growth on a year-over-year basis.

Investors should monitor whether the trend continues as if the top line were to grow faster than expenses, it would imply an extremely efficient business, and that margins haven't topped out. This could dramatically improve sentiment, and the stock could continue trading at a significant premium.

To conclude, Mastercard remains a high-quality financial services and payment processing company. The substantial streak of double-beat performances appears compelling and argues for excellent execution. The top-line and bottom-line grow at a double-digit pace, partially justifying the elevated premium. Margins continue to improve and support the positive outlook. However, based on the significant valuation, the market already knows. This suggests that the future performance may already be priced in, and that also supports my neutral stance, arguing for consolidation in the short term.

Quality Business at a Premium Price

Mastercard trades at a forward P/E of roughly 33x, signaling a 3x premium versus peers at an 11x earnings multiple. The payment processing company also trades at nearly a 44% premium vs. the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23x earnings multiple. MA is a phenomenal business, but the market already knows it.

The chart above suggests that Mastercard has been comfortably trading at a premium valuation for years. Deservedly, too. However, the chart also highlights that MA has been trading in a range from roughly 27x earnings multiple to 38x since the beginning of 2023. This may argue for relatively steady performance, stability, but also stock price appreciation supported by bottom-line growth.

The forward P/E multiple hasn't experienced sharp swings due to strong bottom-line performance and double-digit growth, justifying the premium. The current valuation also marks the higher trading of the range, which signals that consolidation is likely, unless MA accelerates bottom-line expansion or unlocks a new revenue growth cycle.

The forward EV/sales and price/sales ratios of 15.37 and 15.12, respectively, signal substantial overvaluation vs. peers, which is also heavily contributing to my neutral stance. These valuation metrics suggest a 4.8x premium at the midpoint versus the sector medians of 3.18 and 2.99. Such overvaluation supports the thesis that the market has already partially priced in future revenue growth, and if robust performance continues, it may not necessarily translate into meaningful stock price appreciation.

The company has a moderately leveraged capital structure, reflecting $18.98 billion in total obligations. Mastercard has $10.65 billion in cash, which partially offsets the debt, but such leverage results in a substantial financial burden. It paid $747 million in interest expense to service the debt, and that weighed on the bottom-line performance.

As indicated above, MA has been a significant and steady top and bottom-line growth story. Over the past 12 months, the company achieved a 15.60% revenue growth, implying an 85% outperformance vs. peers at 8.43%. The bottom line has grown by 18.23%, reflecting an 18% surge on a year-over-year basis, vs. 15.39%. This validates premium valuation and market-leading position.

Premium margins indicate that Mastercard remains a high-quality business. The company operates at an EBIT margin (TTM) of 59%, signaling a nearly 2.5x outperformance vs. peers at 24%. The net income margin (TTM) of 45% highlights a 91% outperformance vs. the sector median of 24%. If management can sustain such premium margins, and the trend continues, I don't see why MA couldn't trade at an elevated valuation further.

To sum up, Mastercard remains a significant, market-leading business. The company is well managed, delivers steady top- and bottom-line growth, and operates at premium margins, supporting a positive outlook. Although it has a moderately leveraged capital structure, I don't think that is a big issue. The elevated valuation, I believe, is something that clouds significant outperformance, indicating that the future growth may already be priced in the stock price. MA is a high-quality company, but investors know it.

Wall Street forecasts $16.46 in diluted EPS for FY2025, and $19.11 in diluted EPS for FY2026. This reflects a continued growth at a double-digit pace. I think MA remains well positioned to achieve these targets, supported by operations running on all cylinders and solid momentum in demand. I also think the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line expansion, yet also anticipate consolidating a bit. If we apply a 32x earnings multiple, a 4% drop from the current valuation, to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $19.11, we arrive at my price target of $611. This appears to be a reasonable estimate, reflecting a market-like return.

Main Risks and Concerns

Mastercard is tied to risks and concerns that could significantly impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The financial services business is trading at a substantial premium. Although it deserves an elevated valuation, it also caps the future outlook, guiding for consolidation. If sentiment shifts dramatically in the broader market, MA is likely to take a hit alongside other names. This could result in a closer-to-market valuation.

Mastercard is sensitive to the macroeconomic outlook and consumer spending. If the global consumer weakens or economies around the world enter a recession, this could meaningfully impact MA performance. This could result in reduced cross-border payment volumes, weighing on the margins and top-line performance. If that happens, MA is likely to be repriced at a lower valuation.

The company is experiencing fierce competition from Visa and other fintech names, and there's a risk tied to stablecoins, which are reshaping the payment processing industry. However, I think these concerns may appear to be overblown. The rivalry with Visa has been going on for years, and MA is mitigating stablecoin risk by partnering with Circle Internet Group, a USDC issuer, and other stablecoin enablers. In this way, it's bringing blockchain-based payments to its existing merchant network. It will be interesting to see how this plays out, but I argue that MA has done a great job, so far, mitigating the risk.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, MA may continue appreciating in the stock price, followed by solid bottom-line growth.

Mastercard Remains Phenomenal, But the Market Already Knows It

I believe Mastercard remains a significant, industry-leading company, which is positioning itself for future success. It's a well-known brand, has a solid moat and reliable payment network, and with stablecoin adoption may secure the dominant stance. MA is well managed, has been steadily growing its top and bottom line, and performs at market-beating margins.

Although it has a moderately leveraged capital structure, weighing on the bottom-line performance, I don't think that is a significant concern. The elevated valuation, I think, caps the future outlook and supports the thesis of further consolidation. If the company maintains its dominant stance, and double-digit business growth continues, it may appreciate further in the stock price. However, I think it may be challenging to outperform the benchmark without a new catalyst.

I rate Mastercard a Hold with a $611 price target over the next 12 months. This presents a 12% upside possibility, yet market-like returns. I think MA may follow bottom-line performance. 39 Wall Street analysts forecast a $656 price target over the next year, indicating a 21% upside potential. This is possible, but I favor a more conservative approach.

data.title: Market Outlook For 2026: The Fourth Industrial Revolution Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
As part of my due diligence for securities that I am interested in, I follow the macro events that dictate which investments may perform better at various times throughout the economic cycle. Despite all the recent noise about the economy in the U.S., and geopolitical rumblings heard around the world, I am optimistic regarding the stock market in 2026. After all, we are only about 3 years into the latest bull market and tailwinds from the Fourth Industrial Revolution, or 4IR (also called Industry 4.0) are just beginning to propel the S&P 500 (SP500) to new highs in 2025 which I believe will continue for at least another year or two.

The term Fourth Industrial Revolution was coined by Klaus Schwab, Founder and Executive Chairman of the World Economic Forum. He introduced this concept in his book, The Fourth Industrial Revolution, published in 2016. In it, he discusses how emerging technologies like artificial intelligence the Internet of Things, and robotics have begun to merge with the physical, digital and biological worlds and, thus, have revolutionized economies, industries and societies in the process.

History of US Bull and Bear Markets

This chart from First Trust illustrates the historical cycle of bull and bear markets since 1942. The average bull market has lasted 4.3 years while the average bear market lasted just under 1 year.

Yogi Berra once said, “it’s tough to make predictions, especially about the future.” Well life is tough and investors want to know what to expect, so here it is. My prediction is that the S&P 500 will end 2026 at a level about 10% higher than the end of 2025. The current S&P 500 index is right about 6,850 today so I will use that number as the baseline for my prediction, putting my expected ending S&P 500 value as of 12/31/26 at 7,240.

I believe that the drivers for the current bull market are still in place to drive further growth, albeit slower growth than we have experienced over the past 3 years. The current bull market that started in early 2023 still has another year or two left in it in my opinion. The prospect of likely further interest rate cuts should provide some stimulus for that growth as well.

I believe that the growth will slow slightly from 2025 levels in 2026 but the momentum from companies benefiting from the buildout of AI data centers and all the energy and power infrastructure necessary to support all that expansion will continue to drive further growth next year.

The drivers for that mid-range growth of about 10% next year (slower than the 20%+ in 2023 and 2024 and the 16.5% YTD growth) include many of the positive impacts that we are seeing on companies that are benefitting from advances in AI, robotics, virtual reality, quantum computing, the Internet of Things (connected intelligent devices), blockchain, and other advanced Industry 4.0 technologies.

Businesses are undergoing transformation and those companies that are able to innovate and leverage the use of new or advanced technologies in their business models stand to benefit the most from 4IR. The chart below from Research.com suggests that changes in customer expectations are also impacting the digitalization of business models and product innovation.

Growth Stocks Benefiting from 4IR With Momentum Heading into 2026

I have been writing about 4IR and stocks that have shown great growth potential due to the business benefits from the application of Industry 4.0 technologies since March 2023 when I first covered Powell Industries (POWL), calling it a Growth Stock for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Since that initial coverage when I rated POWL stock a Hold, it delivered a total return of nearly 650%.

Despite the big runup in price, POWL gets Buy ratings from both Wall Street and SA Analysts and has very good Quant scores and appears to have more upside ahead with a $1.4B backlog. This comment from CEO Metcalf on the POWL Q4 2025 earnings report summarizes the company outlook for 2026 from their perspective:

"We are confident that the strong commercial momentum we experienced across our key end markets in fiscal 2025 will carry into fiscal 2026."

Another example of a company that has realized benefits from its investments in Industry 4.0 technologies that I first covered in July 2023 is Flex (FLEX), formerly Flextronics International. This was what I wrote in the summary of that article:

  • Flex, formerly known as Flextronics International, is embracing AI and machine learning to optimize manufacturing processes, reduce material loss, and improve customer satisfaction.
  • The company is leveraging Industry 4.0 technologies in areas such as automation, connectivity, and continuous improvement in processes.

FLEX currently gets Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street and expects to see 35% data center revenue growth in FY26 as AI-driven demand accelerates.

Yet another example of a company that I have covered multiple times that has seen benefits from 4IR trends and advancements is Sterling Infrastructure (STRL). I wrote about STRL in February 2024 when I rated the stock a Buy and it has delivered a 282% return since then. This was what I wrote in that article:

Like other small-cap growth companies that I covered in my recent article, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, or Industry 4.0 as it is also called, is driving some of the growth opportunities for STRL. Although the company may not directly offer Industry 4.0 technology as part of their business practices, those trends are at least in part responsible for driving business growth.

Another growth stock that I first covered in July 2024 when I rated it my Best Growth Stock for 2024 is Power Solutions International (PSIX). That stock has also experienced significant outperformance in the past two years due to data center demands and a return to profitability as a result. The stock has appreciated more than 450% since that article was published and is up nearly 100% in the past year.

Although the PSIX stock price plummeted after the Q3 earnings report which included 60% EPS growth, it still appears inexpensive and gets Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street. The CEO indicated a very positive outlook for growth continuing to ramp up in his earnings summary (bold emphasis mine).

Dino Xykis, Chief Executive Officer, said, “We achieved the highest sales in our company’s history this quarter, delivering strong financial performance with sales increasing 62% and net income rising 59%. These results underscore the robust demand for our power systems solutions, particularly within the data center market. During the quarter, we expanded our manufacturing capacity and increased production across key data center product lines. We are continuing to ramp up production to ensure on-time delivery while implementing targeted operational improvements to enhance efficiency, execution and future growth going forward.”

There are other examples of growth stocks that I have not previously covered in detail but that I have been following and that are also seeing benefits from the AI data center buildout and growing demands for the infrastructure to support the power and energy needs of this blossoming industry. A few examples that come to mind include:

Argan, Inc. (AGX), Vertiv Holdings (VRT), Dycom Industries (DY) and Primoris Services (PRIM). The chart below shows the 3-year total return for each of those 4 stocks compared to the S&P 500.

Blockchain Technology and Bitcoin

According to this article on 4IR from Research.com, Klaus Schwab described blockchain as the heart of the Fourth Industrial Revolution.

One of the key features of blockchain technology is that it makes an irreversible timeline of data, as each block is always stored chronologically. Transactions remain visible to all users in the network, and these cannot be changed or altered once added to the shared ledger. This linear, linked structure prevents users from making changes to transactions, making the system tamper-proof. One application of blockchain currently gaining traction is Bitcoin. Experts have explored the potential of Bitcoin and blockchain technology in the shift to a new economic system under the Fourth Industrial Revolution. According to Blockchain co-founder Nicolas Cary, Bitcoin and blockchain can prove crucial to the shift to the circular economy, due to Bitcoin’s ability to perform frictionless, transparent financial transactions without the need for intermediaries.

Whether or not you understand the “value” in bitcoin (BTC-USD), it is here to stay and has been growing in both price and demand over time from financial institutions and intermediaries all over the world. As an investment, Bitcoin has far outpaced the S&P 500 over the past 3 years despite its recent pullback.

In fact, Blackrock recently announced that its Bitcoin holdings have risen more than 250% since the SEC approval of spot Bitcoin ETFs in Q4 2024.

Beyond cryptocurrencies blockchain technology has other important uses as well that are being driven by Industry 4.0 demands including the use of “smart contracts”.

To have the capability to validate, verify, capture, and implement agreements among parties, smart contracts combine with Distributed Ledger Technology and the Industrial Internet of Things. After collecting actual, legal events, a smart contract gathers IoT data—business processes, meters, sensors—to allow for performance assessment. Afterward, this information communicates the automated rules of a contract by posting outcomes and supplemental evidence to the blocks (Schmitt et al., 2019).

Risks and Challenges to 4IR

Beyond the ethical concerns around AI, there are additional potential challenges associated with the Fourth Industrial Revolution that could derail the growth story in the coming years. The rapid pace of technological innovation requires governments to become more “agile”. Governments, including the U.S. and other developed nations in particular, must develop sustainable models for adapting to 4IR. In other words, governments and private industry must work together in making policy and developing regulations to support innovation while minimizing societal impacts.

Businesses must also become more agile and adapt to changing customer expectations. Concerns around privacy and transparency around the use of “big data” need to be addressed. For example, Salesforce (CRM) witnessed the impact of souring consumer sentiment in 2025 around its product offerings despite seeing substantial demand for its AI-centric agentic business model. Even after reporting excellent results in its latest quarterly report and after delivering upbeat guidance for 2026, CRM is the 2nd worst performer in the Dow index in 2025.

"CRM reported its FY3Q26 results with the top-line coming in line with Street expectations while seeing a significant bottom-line beat with better-than-expected FY4Q26 top-line guidance as the company continues to invest aggressively in its Agentforce strategy to reaccelerate growth," said analysts led by Dan Ives.

The analysts added that Salesforce provided fourth quarter fiscal 2026 guidance which came in above Street estimates on the top-line while meeting the Street’s EPS estimate as the company balances investments into growth with margin expansion and free cash flow, or FCF, generation.

"This was a step in the right direction for CRM with a long way to go as the Agentforce strategy builds incremental momentum with a strong pipeline to capitalize on while balancing investments into growth initiatives with bottom-line expansion over the coming quarters," said Ives and his team.

Other risks include the massive investment in AI data centers and the infrastructure needed to support the growing power demands. This recent report from S&P Global highlights those risks.

Data center and AI infrastructure investment has grown rapidly, with its contribution to economic growth already evident. Sector market fundamentals are healthy, and digital infrastructure has become a central theme for investors and lenders. With the AI transformation still in its early stages, we expect this will remain the case for several years, supported by robust demand, limited supply, strong earnings, high prices and narrow corporate spreads.

However, with such capital intensity and investor appetite, competition is also increasing fast. This could lead to higher leverage, unsustainable asset valuations, reduced spread differentiation across the asset-quality spectrum and more aggressive financing structures. These are all typical ingredients for busts and highlight the importance of data center risk management.

Other longer-term risks for data centers include overbuilding, lower residual values, obsolescence, tenant credit quality and regulatory requirements. We believe these risks will not be uniform among market participants should AI revenue promises fade.

The associated risk with all this massive capex spending by many of the big tech companies like Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, Google, and others is that it has led to concentration risk in the S&P 500. The top 10 holdings in the SPY account for nearly 40% of the total market value.

However, as we enter the month of December 2025 there is some sector rotation occurring which broadens the depth of the bull market, offering additional growth opportunity in 2026 for many of the companies outside of the top 10. As fellow analyst, Lawrence Fuller writes, this broadening of the bull market is another investment theme to watch in 2026.

This will be one of my investment themes for 2026, as I see the improvement in earnings growth breadth narrowing the sector performance differential that has been the most outstanding characteristic of this bull market.

Additional macro risks to consider that could influence my prediction for a mildly bullish market in 2026 include:

  • Potential for rising inflation as the US economy re-accelerates.
  • De-globalization of supply chains and manufacturing, contributing to a continued debasement of the USD, leading to a shift toward non-US investments and a further surge in gold prices.
  • AI “bubble” bursting, causing the Mag 7 stocks to suffer a major market correction and impacting the broader market.
  • More private credit “cockroaches” appear and the credit markets see wider spreads leading to a contraction in fixed income investments as risk accelerates.

And of course, there is always the possibility of a “Black Swan” event, something that nobody can predict. If that happens, all bets are off. Meanwhile, I recommend staying invested and do not veer from your investment strategy unless you are completely dependent on NVDA stock going to the stratosphere. In that case, you may want to adjust your strategy to account for that concentration risk.

data.title: S&P 500: Junk Bonds Indicate Prolonged Bull Run
data.description:
S&P 500: why junk bonds are a breadth signal

Like many of you, my inbox is flooded by bearish calls on the equity market as all equity indices (including SP500, DJI, NASDAQ, and also RTY) hover near all-time-highs. These bearish calls are supported by various reasons that can cause an “imminent market correction.” These reasons range from old-fashioned high P/E to more exotic ones like the high Buffett Indicator, worsening market liquidity, the excessive concentration on the magnificent 7 stocks, the rule of 20, etc. I’ve published a few articles recently to closely examine these arguments and explain why they can misleading (for example, my latest one is titled “S&P 500: Rule Of 20 Likely To Remain Misleading In 2026”).

After writing several of these, the intellectual stimulation of making counterarguments began to wear off. Deconstructing old things can be fun for a while, but eventually constructing something new is more lasting – and also rewarding - intellectually. As such, in this article, instead keep explaining why bears’ thesis are flawed, I will present an alternative market indicator that I have been using over the years (and with good success fortunately): junk bond yield. The remainder of this article will explain A) why and how I use junk bond yields to assess the breadth of a bull market, and B) why what I believe current junk bond yields point to a prolonged bull run.

With that, let me directly get to the point with the following chart. The chart details Moody’s seasoned BAA corporate bond in the past 20 years. As seen, the current yield is 5.85%, which is slightly above the average yield over this period (indicated by the dashed line at 5.37%), a period long enough to roughly span a complete macro debt cycle in my view. As seen, the yield has reached almost 10% in late 2008 or early 2009, reflecting the financial crisis. This peak was followed by a secular decline and bottomed at around 3% amid the epic easing after the COVID pandemic. The yield has broadly increased since then more or less reverted to the long-term mean.

Such a more-or-less normal yield for BAA bonds sends two signals to me. First, it indicates that the prevailing sentiment toward corporate credit and liquidity is normal (and normal means healthy in my book). Second, it also indicates to me that investors’ optimism has not gone too extreme. I am not too concerned when investors are willing to pay a high premium for assets with higher quality, and I am very concerned when the enthusiasm extends to assets with sub-par quality (such as those with BAA rating or lower).

Junk bond yield

At this point, some careful readers probably are already yelling at me – but Moody's seasoned Baa corporate bonds are not considered junk bonds! Indeed, the definition of junk bond is vague to start with. Bonds with credit ratings below the investment grade threshold are often called junk bonds. Different rating agencies use different terminologies and picture can become confusing. In this article, I define junk bonds as those with credit ratings of BB+ and lower in S&P or Fitch terminologies or Ba1 and lower in Moody's. By this definition, the BAA yields cited above do not represent junk bond yield. But they do represent corporate bonds with weaker credit ratings, and their yields correlate closely with those with even lower ratings as shown in the next picture below.

To wit, the next chart below shows the yield from the Vanguard High-Yield Corporate Fund Investor Shares (VWEHX) in the past 30 years. VWEHX is the oldest high-yield bond mutual fund that I can find with an inception date in 1978 and thus provides plenty of historical data for this analysis. As seen, VWEHX yield has changed in very similar patters as the BAA corporate bond yield shows above, with a peak around 2008~2009, a bottom near 2022~2023, and a current reading very close to the long-term mean (6.15% vs. 6.19%).

And VWEHX’s holdings are well-aligned with my above definition of junk bond. As seen in the second p picture below, VWEHX is heavily exposure to sub-par bonds with ratings below BBB. Its largest holdings are rated BB, accounting for almost ½ (or 49.67% to be exact) of its total assets. The next largest holdings are rated B, accounting for about 1/3 of its total assets.

BAA credit spread

Another reason that I am not alarmed – at least in the near term - is the credit yield spread between BAA and risk-free rates. To wit, the chart below displays the spread between Moody’s Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield Relative to the Yield on 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity in the past 5 years. This spread represents the extra yield investors demand to hold lower-tier corporate debt compared to the risk-free 10-Year Treasury, thus serving as another indicator for investors risk appetite.

The spread has fluctuated between around 2.4% and 1.4% during this periods. The current 1.76% reading sits slightly below my estimated five-year average of 1.9%, which can signal a certain degree of risk appetite of market participants. However, I believe the appetite is quite moderate and largely reflects the anticipation of the rate cuts based on the latest announcements made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). Based on FOMC’s latest comments and dot-plot, I see good odds for 1 or 2 rate cuts in the next 1~2 years total 25 to 50 basis points. These cuts, if materialized, could potentially widen the credit yield between BAA bonds and 10-year treasury rates to a range of 2.01% and 2.26%, slightly above the 5-year average.

Other risks and final thoughts

Let me close by iterating a key word I used in my above projection – near term. My above outlook is meant for the next 1~2 years based on my judgment of current macroeconomic condition and Fed’s dot-plot. In the longer term, tighter credit spread does show a close correlation with market downturns and vice versa – that is why I monitor junk bond yields and their spread relative to risk-free rates in the first place.

As an example, the last figure shows the long-term relationship between the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) price movement (top panel) and the VWEHX yield spread relative to 10-year treasury rates (the bottom panel). Thanks to both SPY and VWEHX’s long history, we have data to examine their correlation over several market cycles. As seen, even a casual look can reveal an inverse correlation between SPY price movements and the spread between VWEHX and treasury rates. I added some red and green arrows to further highlight several key market junctures. The red arrows show that surges in the spread have often been followed by large SPY price rallies. Vice versa, deep SPY price corrections have been often preceded by below average spread, and the current spread is below the average observed in this long period.

For me, taking an average for this such a long period (of almost 5 decades) can be misleading as I believe future interest rates won’t (or cannot) be as high as those observed before 2000 (as detailed in my latest article on the Rule Of 20).

To close, the goal of this article is to caution readers about the various “market peak indictors” I see more and more often these days in my inbox. These indicators all have limitations and could be misleading under current market conditions. Given what I see, specially in terms of junk bond yields, my verdict is that the risk of a drastic allocation away from equity is far larger than the risks of staying invested under current conditions. In my own accounts and also our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement), I apply a dynamic allocation model to perform gradual and disciplined adjustment of equity-bond ratio based on their relative valuation using metrics discussed in this article. There are of course other alternative/better approaches, but my point is that it is much better to follow a disciplined approach rather than keep guessing the market top.

data.title: Stoke Therapeutics: A Long-Term Biotech Growth Play - Why I Assign A Hold Rating
data.description:
Stoke Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ: STOK) stock is enjoying a great year. The past 6 months saw its stock gaining over 180%. Such gains could be already pricing in Phase 3 success of its lead pipeline. The company isn't just developing a drug but pioneering a new class of medicine. Its platform has the potential to redefine treatment for rare diseases and I think the company stands on the verge of a major breakthrough. I, however, assign a HOLD rating on the stock considering the long way towards pipeline success.

Stoke's Business Model

Stoke is a developer of RNA-based medicines for rare genetic diseases like Dravet syndrome. The company's lead drug candidate, zorevunersen, is currently in Phase 3 trials. The company currently funds its operations through R&D collaborations plus cash reserves. Looking into the future, the company's customers will be patients and payments will be coming from healthcare systems should the drug be approved.

Assessing Pipeline

Stock's pipeline is promising led by its zorevunersen drug. The drug is in its pivotal Phase 3 trial and has shown strong early enrollment. The latest update on the drug is that its four years of data is showing not just reduced seizures but also durable cognitive improvements. The disease-modifying potential the drug carries is a potential blockbuster in an around 38,000 patient market.

Stoke also has early-stage programs for two rare genetic disorders. STK-002 for ADOA, an optic nerve disease, is in Phase 1. Meanwhile, the SYNGAP1 (a neurodevelopmental disorder) program is still in preclinical research. Interestingly, both programs are applying the same clever science of boosting protein expression from the healthy gene copy. I view this strategy as a high-risk but the science is quite compelling.

The TANGO Platform

Stoke has a very unique proprietary technology, Targeted Augmentation of Nuclear Gene Output (TANGO). If approved, zorevunersen's success in Dravet syndrome will be a key validation of the entire TANGO platform.

Stoke carries the potential to transform from a one-asset bet into a credible platform company with a repeatable playbook for treating haploinsufficiency diseases. I believe that the company's long-term value lies in this proven engine for drug creation.

Brief Q3 Update

Stoke reported a good set of Q3 results illustrating a perfect pre-revenue biotech entity executing its strategy well. The company reported a revenue beat but not driven by product sales but rather collaboration funding from Biogen. The company received payment from Biogen for licensing rights and to share development costs for its lead drug outside of North America.

That said, the real story is the company's financial fortitude behind its science. Stoke ended the quarter with a strong $328 million in cash. This was even after a significant operational burn. The company even reported that this cash position provides a runway into mid-2028.

The Biogen Collaboration

Earlier this year, Stoke entered into a biotech licensing deal which saw it selling its commercial rights to its lead drug outside North America to Biogen.

I believe this deal gave the company some massive advantages and that the drug's potential has been maximised.

That said, there is a potential downside. The company could be sacrificing long-term international revenue for near-term financial stability and development support. Stoke's bet is that teaming up with Biogen's global power gives it a better chance of success in North America than trying to go it alone.

In another thinking, this Biogen deal somehow sets the stage for a future buyout. Biogen will be left owning the global rights except for North America, assuming the drug succeeds. This could appear a bit messy for the company and buying the whole company could be a simple fix on Biogen's part. This makes Stoke a potential future acquisition target. I believe that this adds a potential M&A premium to the company's story beyond just the drug's sales potential.

Competition

The company's main competitor isn't just another drug but the entire gene-therapy approach. Its main competitor is Encoded Therapeutics which is also targeting Dravet's root cause. It's doing so through advancing a one-time gene-regulating therapy, ETX101. Encoded has already reported promising early clinical data.

Stoke's moat hinges on its modality. Because it’s an antisense oligonucleotide, zorevunersen can be given repeatedly and adjusted in dose. I believe that this offers a crucial safety advantage when compared with permanent and one-time gene therapy. Should long-term data continue to show not just seizure reduction but durable cognitive gains, I think that reversible benefit could become its unassailable moat. This could make the drug a preferred choice for the lifelong neurological condition.

There is also a massive commercial advantage to Stoke's model that appeals to both investors and payers. Its chronic annuity model is much more manageable than a one-time cure model which brings with it a multi-million-dollar price tag. That could terrify payers. Stoke's model makes it far easier for insurance companies to budget for and to pay. I believe that this makes it a much smoother and more reliable business.

Other Key Risks to Watch

The company is also faced with other real risks beyond that of competition. Its Phase 3 trial could simply fail to hit its endpoints convincingly. Should that happen, it'll be left with great science but with zero marketable product. Also, regulatory pathways are not as predictable. The FDA might wake up demanding more data thereby delaying launch for more years.

The other question relates to commercialisation. The company's ability to then be able to build a sales force and secure reimbursement to reach patients is an area of scrutiny as it can leave the company exposed. The bottom line is that a breakthrough therapy is worthless if it fails to get to the people who need it.

Valuation

The company's forward P/E of 656.77 is 3,287.42% higher than the sector median of 19.39. This valuation is not based on earnings fundamentals because such are non-existent. Its entirely based on speculative pipeline potential. It appears this P/E level versus the sector is showing that the market has already priced in a zorevunersen Phase 3 success. I think this leaves the stock overvalued with no margin for error. Any setback would potentially result in a sharp correction.

Conclusion

I think Stoke's story presents a compelling opportunity. The company's validated platform, a cash runway through key milestones makes the path very clear. This position is also cemented by a solid strategic partner in Biogen. I believe that the potential for both commercial success and a strategic buyout are both high. That said, I still think Stoke is a high-risk, high-reward opportunity. Its pipeline justifies investor optimism but not a new buy at current levels. I assign a HOLD rating on the stock.

data.title: AI Bubble Or Supercycle? AMD CEO Says Quiet Part Out Loud
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Lisa Su recently spoke at UBS’s Global Technology and AI Conference.

The Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) CEO doubled down on the idea that we are not in an AI bubble, but rather “two years into a ten-year super cycle” of compute demand.

Moreover, she laid out why AMD is structurally positioned to capture a double-digit share of a now $1 trillion data center total addressable market, or TAM, by 2030.

The conversation reinforced several pillars of my original AMD thesis.

First, that AMD’s pivot to high-performance computing and AI is deliberate, multi-year, and working.

Secondly, the chiplet-first architecture and rack-scale systems offer a genuine competitive advantage.

And, most importantly, that AI is lifting both accelerators and CPUs, creating a second wave of upside that he market still isn’t fully pricing in.

In short, this UBS appearance made me even more confident that AMD can grow into a trillion-dollar valuation over the next cycle.

I continue to see a path to $775 per share and, in a bull case, something closer to $1,000.

From “CPU Underdog” To Data Centre Powerhouse

The first thing Su hammered home is that AMD is no longer a PC/CPU underdog trying to claw back share. It is now structurally a data center company.

Over the last few years, AMD has deliberately reoriented its R&D and product roadmaps around high-performance computing and AI.
