We’ve been incredibly focused on high-performance computing… and then a few years ago, it became absolutely clear that this was going to be all about AI… So yes, we’ve pivoted our R&D capabilities… to a significant focus on high-performance computing and AI.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

We can't deny that this pivot has worked so far.

AMD’s data center business has been growing over 50% a year and, according to Su, is set to accelerate from “50% plus… to over 60% plus as we go forward.”

Data center has gone from under 20% of the business to nearly half.

$1T TAM With Double-Digit Share Ambitions

At its Analyst Day, AMD lifted its data center TAM estimate to $1 trillion by 2030, up from $500 billion by 2028. At UBS, Su reiterated not just the TAM, but their share ambition.

You really have to have a holistic view… CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, the possibility of doing ASICs, it’s being able to integrate all of that together. And that’s our unique capability… we’re the only semiconductor company out there that has all of this foundational IP.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

This claim is not unsubstantiated, but rather well grounded in a portfolio that spans CPUs, GPUs and semi-custom / ASIC-like work, all tied together by common IP and a chiplet-based design philosophy.

Chiplets are how you mix and match compute, I/O, and memory without restarting from scratch each cycle, which provides a structural edge in a world where workloads, models and memory requirements are shifting every year.

ASICs vs GPUs: Why Lisa Thinks GPUs Still Win This Cycle

One of the more interesting exchanges was about whether custom ASICs will “eat” the GPU accelerator market.

Su’s view is nuanced, and she gave credit where credit is due.

Google has done a great job with the TPU architecture… But it is a more purpose-built architecture. It’s not built with the same programmability… the same capabilities to do training and inference where GPUs are.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Models and workloads are changing fast, and developers want maximum flexibility. That favors programmable GPUs, not fixed-function ASICs.

Our view is… over the next 5 years or so, we’ll see GPUs still be the significant majority of the market because we are still so early in the cycle and because software developers actually want the flexibility to innovate on different algorithms.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Lisa Su still sees ASICs as 20–25% of the market, which is exactly the framework I’ve been using.

ASICs will grow, but they are a complement, not a death sentence for GPUs. For AMD, that’s extremely important. It means the sweet spot of the market, general-purpose, high-performance accelerators, remains attractive and large enough for a second major vendor.

From Silicon To Systems: Helios, ZT and Rack-Scale AI

Another key theme from the UBS talk is AMD’s ongoing transition from being “just a chip company” to being a systems company, something I highlighted after the Q3 print with the Helios platform.

Su was very explicit about why AMD is doing this:

The reason we’re doing this integration is the time to useful capability… is super critical. So the full-stack solution is a way for us to help customers get to productive compute capacity.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

The ZT Systems acquisition and Sanmina partnership give AMD the ability to offer rack-level, reference designs like Helios, which integrate MI400/MI450 GPUs, EPYC CPUs and high-speed networking into an open, Meta-co-designed rack architecture.

Best-in-class system design… combined with our best-in-class hardware and software capability… We’re super excited about MI450 and the Helios product that will come to market in 2026.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

This is exactly how Nvidia (NVDA) built its moat. They don’t just sell chips; the offer a whole ecosystem.

AMD is now following the same playbook, but with an open rack ecosystem that hyperscalers and cloud providers may eventually prefer.

AI Is Not A Bubble, It’s A 10-Year Super Cycle

The other big takeaway was Su’s stance on the “AI bubble” debate. She was blunt:

There’s not a concept of a bubble. What there is a concept of is, we are, let’s call it, 2 years into a 10-year super cycle.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

From her vantage point, talking to “the largest AI users out there”, the constraint isn’t optimism, it’s compute:

The one thing that is constant as we talk to customers is we need more compute… if there was more compute installed… we would get to the answer faster.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

On ROI, she pointed to AMD’s own internal experience over the past 15–18 months:

What started as ‘let’s try AI for our internal use cases’ has now turned into significant clear productivity wins… there is no question that there is a return on investment for investment in AI.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

AI capex is not pure speculation; it is becoming embedded in productivity, product development and service delivery. The gains are definitely there, even if not for everyone, at least the big tech companies seem to be benefiting from an AI productivity boost.

CPUs, PCs and AI Agents

One of the more under-appreciated parts of the conversation was about CPUs and PCs. Su pushed back on the idea that GPUs would “take over the world” and leave CPUs structurally disadvantaged.

Instead, she argued that AI is actually reviving CPU demand:

Over the last 3 months, what we’ve seen is really a significant uptick in CPU demand… as AI moves to more inferencing and there’s more work being done in things like agent workloads… they’re spawning more general-purpose CPU needs.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Her framing is simple but powerful.

Basically, if you have “1,000 agents or 1,000 virtual employees,” they need somewhere to run, operate on data, and do work, and that’s general-purpose CPU territory.

AI isn’t just a GPU story for AMD. It touches every part of AMD’s portfolio, accelerators, CPUs, and even higher-end PCs.

Risks: Supply, Power, Competition

None of this is risk-free.

Su highlighted issues which we have already flagged.

Limitations of memory, CoWoS packaging, and increasingly, power availability in data center regions.

However, the CEO was reasonably confident AMD can secure what it needs, pointing to deep partnerships with TSMC, memory vendors and packagers, and noting that governments are moving to accelerate power build-outs.

Final Thoughts

This UBS appearance was, in many ways, a live confirmation of the thesis I laid out in my last AMD article.

AMD has deliberately repositioned itself around high-performance computing and AI.

In doing so, it has built a unique, chiplet-driven portfolio that spans CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs and semi-custom, while moving up the stack into rack-scale systems with Helios and ZT.

I still see a realistic path to my $775 base case with EPS approaching $15 and a 25x multiple, and a credible bull scenario closer to $1,000 per share if AMD executes on its share ambitions in a $1 trillion data center market.

While some companies in AI are overpromising and thrashing, AMD is doing something far more valuable: it is quietly executing.
